coredata.dc:title: Switched photoelectrochemistry of carbon dots for split-type immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Yu-Ting
Yuan, Fang
Dong, Yuming
Li, Zaijun
Wang, Guang-Li
coredata.dc:description: A novel and general strategy of split-type immunoassay is developed based on redox chemical reaction modulated photoelectrochemistry of carbon dots (CDs). Specifically, the photocurrent of the CDs sensitized titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) modified fluorine doped indium tin oxide (FTO) (that is the FTO/TiO2/CDs) electrode was inhibited obviously by KMnO4 due to the oxidation of surface hydroxyl groups of CDs to electron accepting carbonyls. While the inhibited photocurrent of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode can be restored by ascorbic acid (AA) because of the regeneration of electron donating hydroxyls to promote electron-hole separation. Take carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a model analyte and alkaline phosphate (ALP) as a catalytic label tracer to hydrolyze ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) for producing AA, which greatly stimulated the photocurrent of the transducer of KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs photoelectrode for signal output. This redox chemical reaction modulated PEC strategy enabled the separation of the immunoreaction from the photoelectrode (that is, a split-type PEC detection), eliminating potential damage of biomolecules during the PEC detection processes and leading to enhanced throughput detection as compared to conventional PEC configurations. A low detection limit of 7.0 fg/mL was achieved for CEA. This convenient, split-type PEC immunoassay with high throughput may be easily extended to other bioaffinity assays for versatile targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Split photoelectrochemistry
Immunoassay
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modulated PEC response of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode by KMnO4/AA and its mechanism
Split PEC immunoassay for CEA based on the switched photoelectrochemistry of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photocurrent responses of the FTO electrode modified by TiO2 and TiO2/CDs, and the photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode was suppressed by 5.0 × 10−5 mol/L KMnO4 and then restored by the subsequent reaction with 5.0 × 10−4 mol/L AA. (B) The full-scan XPS spectrum of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode, and the XPS spectrum of Ti 2p (C), O 1s (D), and C 1s (E) for the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode. The XPS spectrum of C 1s for CDs on the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode treated by 5.0 × 10−5 mol/L KMnO4 (F) and then by 5.0 × 10−4 mol/L AA (G).

Fluorescence responses of the CDs' solution is quenched by 5.0 × 10−5 mol/L KMnO4 and then restored by 5.0 × 10−4 mol/L AA. (Insets: the corresponding images of CDs under UV light before (a) and after the sequential reaction with KMnO4 (b) and AA (c)).

(A) Effect of different concentrations of ALP as a catalyst for the hydrolysis of AAP on the photocurrent of FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode treated by KMnO4, ALP of elevated concentrations corresponding to (a–j): 0, 5.0 × 10−3, 5.0 × 10−2, 0.1, 0.5, 5.0, 20, 50, 1.0 × 102, 1.5 × 102 U/L respectively, (B) The relationship between the relative photocurrent increment ((I-I0)/I0) and the logarithmical concentration of ALP. (where I0 and I are the photocurrent intensity of KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode in the absence and presence of AA respectively), (C) Response of the detection system to ALP (1.0 U/L) in comparison with other interferents: 5 μg/L for GOx, trypsin, thrombin, BSA; 0.5 mmol/L for K+, Na+,Ca2+, GSH, Cys.

(A) Effect of different concentrations of CEA on the photocurrent responses of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode, CEA of elevated concentrations corresponding to (a–j): 0, 1.0 × 10−2, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 50, 5.0 × 102, 2.0 × 103, 5.0 × 103, 3.0 × 104 pg/mL respectively. (B) The relationship between the relative photocurrent increment ((I-I0)/I0) and the logarithmical concentration of CEA, the error bars indicate the relative standard deviation of four repeated experiments. (C) Selectivity of the immunoassay to CEA (0.01 ng/mL) compared with other interferents at 0.1 ng/mL level: human IgG, goat IgG, AFP, PSA, BSA and HCG.

Schematic diagram of the split-type PEC immunoassay for CEA with immunogold labeled ALP for the catalyzed AA production in the microplates (A), the redox reaction modulated surface states of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode by KMnO4 and AA (B), and the signal generation mechanism of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode (C).
Method accuracy and recovery evaluation of the developed PEC immunoassay for CEA in human serum samples with the reference ELISA kit.

row.entry: method accuracy; conc.[mean ± SD(RSD),ng/mL, n = 3]; t-test

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CEA ELISA kit was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The texp values could be figured by means of a t-test statistical analysis between two methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CEA standards with different densities were added into the preceding human serum samples, respectively.

head.ce:title: Switched photoelectrochemistry of carbon dots for split-type immunoassay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Center for Photoresponsive Molecules and Materials
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and general strategy of split-type immunoassay is developed based on redox chemical reaction modulated photoelectrochemistry of carbon dots (CDs). Specifically, the photocurrent of the CDs sensitized titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) modified fluorine doped indium tin oxide (FTO) (that is the FTO/TiO2/CDs) electrode was inhibited obviously by KMnO4 due to the oxidation of surface hydroxyl groups of CDs to electron accepting carbonyls. While the inhibited photocurrent of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode can be restored by ascorbic acid (AA) because of the regeneration of electron donating hydroxyls to promote electron-hole separation. Take carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a model analyte and alkaline phosphate (ALP) as a catalytic label tracer to hydrolyze ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) for producing AA, which greatly stimulated the photocurrent of the transducer of KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs photoelectrode for signal output. This redox chemical reaction modulated PEC strategy enabled the separation of the immunoreaction from the photoelectrode (that is, a split-type PEC detection), eliminating potential damage of biomolecules during the PEC detection processes and leading to enhanced throughput detection as compared to conventional PEC configurations. A low detection limit of 7.0 fg/mL was achieved for CEA. This convenient, split-type PEC immunoassay with high throughput may be easily extended to other bioaffinity assays for versatile targets.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemistry, a recently emerged and fast-growing analytical method derived from electrochemistry, inherits attractive merits from the traditional electrochemical methods [1–3]. The electronic readout makes it simpler, cheaper, and easier to miniaturize than that of the traditionally optical detections. In addition, the different forms of energy for excitation (light) and detection (electricity) effectively reduces background noise, offering its potentially high sensitivity [4]. As a result, photoelectrochemical (PEC) bioassays, especially the PEC immunoassays, are now receiving increasingly considerable interest due to their potential applications in various clinical diagnosis [5–9]. Usually, the PEC immunoassays will integrate natural enzymes as the catalytic tracer for signal generation/amplification to achieve high sensitivity. Nevertheless, the conventionally PEC immunoassays are accustomed to fixing the biomolecules (antibodies, antigens, and enzymes labeled antibodies) on the electrode surface, which can inevitably lead to denaturation of biomolecules during the PEC detection processes because the light irradiation (especially by the UV light) as well as the destructive species (photogenerated holes, hydroxyl radicals, superoxide anion radicals, and so on) produced by the photoelectrochemically active materials [10–13] may cause damage to biomolecules. Besides, the immobilized biomolecules (usually with a large size) would not only absorb or scatter the excitation light but also sterically hinder the interaction between the transducer (the photoelectrochemically active materials modified photoelectrodes) and the enzymatically generated signal reporter (electron receptors/donors for the photoelectrochemically active materials), crippling accuracy and sensitivity of the detection. In view of an urgent demand to solve the above problems, a split-type PEC detection that separating the biological reaction from the photoelectrode is recently proposed [14]. However, this pioneering work emphasizes the realization of split detection by means of specially designed equipment (a pump) to transfer the enzymatically generated products from the microplates to the PEC detection cell [15], which was no help for improving selectivity of the detection because all the matrix/reactants/products were introduced in the PEC detection cell. What's worse, rinsing the PEC detection cell to eliminate the former residues is necessary before each injection of a new sample, which results in tedious, time-consuming procedure and a large consumption of washing solutions. Thus, to develop other more exquisitely split-type PEC bioassays is still demanding but challenging.

To a large extent, the PEC materials that transducing the signal determine the performances of the PEC detections. Semiconducting quantum dots (QDs), such as CdS [16], CdSe [17], CdTe [18] and PbS [19], have been extensively utilized in PEC bioanalysis. Unfortunately, all of these materials contain heavy metals, whose toxicity seems to be a key question both for their in vivo applications and the operation/environment safety concern. Moreover, the difficulties in tailoring the PEC properties of these semiconducting QDs might impede their utilization in advanced sensing applications. As a kind of eco-friendly and metal-free material, carbon dots (CDs), have emerged as a new star in scientific research. Not only are CDs simple to synthesis, but also they are biocompatible, water dispersible, earth abundant, chemically inert, photostable against photobleaching and blinking [20–22]. Unfortunately, at present, few people has explored the CDs as a PEC transducer in PEC analysis [23].

Herein, we develop a novel strategy for the construction of split-type PEC immunoassay on the basis of redox chemical reaction modulated surface states and the resultant switched photoelectrochemistry of CDs. That is, the anodic photocurrent of CDs sensitized TiO2 NPs modified fluorine tin oxide (FTO) electrode (that is the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode) is inhibited due to oxidation the surface hydroxyl groups of CDs by KMnO4 to electron accepting carbonyls, which facilitates the electron-hole recombination. While the inhibited photocurrent can be restored due to the reduction of carbonyls to electron donating hydroxyl groups, which can effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Taking carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a model analyte and alkaline phosphate (ALP) as a catalytic label tracer, a split-type PEC immunoassay was realized, in which the immune reaction was conducted in the microwell plates and the enzymatic hydrolysates of ascorbic acid (AA) (produced by ALP catalyzes the hydrolysis and transphosphorylation of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate, AAP) effectively stimulated the anodic photocurrent (recorded in the conventional PEC detection cell) of KMnO4 quenched FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode. It was proven to be highly sensitive and selective for the detection of CEA (with the detection limit of 7.0 fg/mL) resulted from the fact that it avoided the adverse effect of the immobilized biomolecules on electrode for crippling the signal readout. In addition, it has enhanced throughput in comparison with the conventional PEC immunoassys because of the introduction of microplates and the separated sample incubation from the signal record. It opens up a new way for the construction of split PEC detection, which is more facile and reagent/time-saving than the reported one [15] without the need of complicated device and copious washing procedures.

Ethylenediamine, citric acid, KMnO4, ascorbic acid (AA), and trisodium citrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The TiO2 NPs (P25) with an average particle size of 25 nm was purchased from Evonik Industries (formerly Degussa, Frankfurt, Germany). Alkaline phosphate (ALP), ascorbic acid 2–phosphate (AAP) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), anti-carcinoembryonic antibody (capture antibody, Ab1, and detection antibody, Ab2) were obtained from Jorferin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements were conducted by a homemade PEC system, which was composed of a 500 W Xe lamp (equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ ≥ 400 nm) to provide the irradiation source) and a CHI 800C electrochemical workstation (to record photocurrent). Photocurrent was measured using a three-electrode system at a constant potential of 0.1 V (vs saturated Ag/AgCl): a modified FTO electrode with an average area of 0.25 cm2 utilized as the working electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode and a Pt wire used as the counter electrode.

Water–soluble CDs were synthesized by a one–pot hydrothermal method according to a previous report [24]. In the hydrothermal process, 5.25 g of citric acid, 1.675 mL of ethylenediamine, and 50 mL of ultrapure water were mixed in a 100 mL glass beaker under vigorous stirring to obtain a clear and colorless solution. Subsequently, about 50 mL of the mixture was transferred to the stainless steel autoclave and kept in an oven at 200 °C for 5 h. When cooled down by water or naturally, a brown-black solution was obtained, indicating the formation of the CDs. The solution was further dialyzed for 48 h to obtain a reddish brown and transparent product. Finally, the obtained water–soluble CDs were stored at 4 °C for future use.

To clean the fluorine doped indium tin oxide (FTO) slices, they were immersed in 2 mol/L boiling KOH solution dissolved in isopropyl alcohol for 20 min, followed by washing with water thoroughly and dried at 120 °C for 2 h.

The TiO2 modified FTO electrodes (FTO/TiO2) were fabricated by the addition of 30 μL of the 1 mg/mL TiO2 (P25) NPs' solution on the FTO electrode, followed by drying in air and calcination at 450 °C for 30 min in a muffle furnace. For getting CDs sensitized FTO/TiO2 electrode (FTO/TiO2/CDs), the FTO/TiO2 electrodes were immersed into CDs' solutions for 1 h and then followed by careful washing and drying at room temperature for future use.

Considering that inorganic phosphate could suppress the activity of ALP [25], we utilized Tris-HCl rather than the phosphate as the buffer solution. And the dilution and usage of ALP should be with 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl (pH 8.0) buffer containing 0.2 mmol/L ZnCl2 and 5 mmol/L MgCl2. The preparation of antigen (Ag) and antibody (Ab) stock solutions used 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4). In the immunoassay, the washing buffer solution was 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.05% Tween-20. The blocking buffer was 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl containing 3% (w/v) BSA.

To conduct the immunoassay, Au NPs with co-immobilized ALP and the anti-CEA antibody (the second antibody, Ab2) (i.e., the Au NPs/Ab2/ALP bioconjugates) were prepared as the signal label tracer. Firstly, the Au NPs were prepared according to a previous protocol [26]. That is, 2.5 mL 1% trisodium citrate solution was added to 100 mL of 0.01% boiled HAuCl4 solution under vigorous stirring, and the color of the reaction solution changed from colorless to deep red. After cooling down the reaction mixture under continued stirring, the product of Au NPs were obtained and stored in dark at 4 °C for future use. Subsequently, to immobilize ALP and anti-CEA antibody (Ab2) on Au NPs [27,28], 20 μL of 0.5 mg/mL Ab2 and 40 μL of 0.5 mg/mL ALP were added simultaneously to the Au NPs' solution, followed by incubation for 2 h under gentle stirring. Afterwards, 200 μL of 1% BSA blocking solution was added and incubated for another 30 min. The Au NPs/Ab2/ALP were centrifuged at 7000 rpm/min and washed thoroughly with washing buffer solution for three times. Finally, the Au NPs/Ab2/ALP bioconjugates were redispersed in 200 μL of 1% BSA blocking solution for further use.

A 25 μL of 0.5 mg/mL anti-CEA antibody (capture antibody, Ab1) was firstly spread onto the 96 well plates and incubated at 4 °C overnight followed by rinsing with the washing buffer solution thoroughly. In the following step, the plates were blocked with 25 μL of the blocking buffer solution for 2 h to block nonspecific binding sites, followed by thorough washing. Subsequently, 25 μL of different concentrations of CEA were added and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, and then washed with the washing buffer. After the immune reaction between Ab1 and CEA, the Au NPs/Ab2/ALP bioconjugates were introduced in the plates for a reaction time of 1 h at 37 °C, followed by washing and the addition of 50 μL of 0.5 mg/mL AAP solution and 50 μL of 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl (pH = 8.0). Finally, the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrodes were immersed into the solution of the well plates and allowed for 25 min incubation at room temperature. Finally, the electrodes were taken out followed by washing and the PEC measurements in 0.1 mol/L Tris-HCl (pH 7.0) buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulated PEC response of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode by KMnO4/AA and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The size and composition of the synthesized CDs were characterized. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. S1A, Supporting Information) indicates that the average diameter of the prepared CDs was about 3.3 ± 0.4 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1B) with the broad peak at 24° depicts that the crystal state of CDs is an amorphous topography [29]. The functional groups on the surface of CDs were confirmed from the Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopy (Fig. S1C), whose features describe a broad band at around 3410 cm−1 from the vibration of O–H, a traditional band at 1637 cm−1 in accordance with the stretching vibrations of C=O.

Fig. 1 A depicts the photocurrent response of the FTO/TiO2 electrode before/after loading with CDs under visible light illumination. Clearly, the greatly enhanced photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode in comparison with that of the FTO/TiO2 electrode implies not only the successful CDs loading but also the effective coupling between CDs and TiO2. The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of FTO/TiO2 and FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode (Fig. S2A) helped us to understand the above PEC responses. Because of the wide band gap, the TiO2 only absorbs ultraviolet light and thus hardly shows any PEC response under the visible light excitation. However, after sensitized by CDs, the FTO/TiO2 electrode shows an obviously PEC response in the visible region because the capacity of CDs for visible strong light absorption. At the same time, the photocurrent stability of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode is also displayed in Fig. S2B. The photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode did not decrease during the repetitive excitation cycles, demonstrating good photophysical stability of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode. As can be seen in Fig. 1B–E, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode demonstrates the corresponding XPS spectra of Ti 2p, O 1S and C 1s, verifying the successful conjunction of CDs on TiO2. The Ti 2p includes of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 at around 464 and 458 eV, respectively, while the O 1s signal is located at about 530 eV. No Ti−C bond is found meaning that CDs are physically adsorbed rather than chemisorbed on the surface of TiO2. According to the fitted XPS results of C 1S (Fig. 1E–G), there are three peaks at 284.6, 286.7, and 287.3 eV, which are in accordance with the C=C, C-O, and C=O bonds, respectively [30].

As noticed, after treated by KMnO4, the photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode was inhibited seriously; while the inhibited photocurrent can be recovered after the subsequent reaction of KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode with ascorbic acid (AA). More in-depth investigations were made to classify the mechanism of the switched PEC response of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode by KMnO4/AA. The XPS results revealed the reversible redox reaction occurred at the surface functional groups of CDs upon the addition of KMnO4 and AA (Fig. 1E–G). The intensity of the C=O peak at 288.2 eV raised, while the C-OH peak at 286.5 eV was suppressed apparently after the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode was treated by KMnO4, indicating that KMnO4 disrupted the surface hydroxyl groups of CDs and oxidized the hydroxyl to carbonyl groups. On the contrary, the destructed surface states of CDs by KMnO4 was restored by AA on the basis of its reduction for the carbonyl groups to hydroxyl groups. This was revealed by the fact that the recovered intensity of C-OH peak at 286.5 eV and the suppressed intensity of the C=O peak at 288.2 eV after the subsequent reaction of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode with AA. Considering whether the reduction product of KMnO4 such as MnO2 presumably cause photocurrent decrease of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode, we also chosen XPS to elaborate the existence of MnO2. Nevertheless, no obvious signal for Mn element was seen in XPS, excluding the role of MnO2 in photocurrent inhibition. Furthermore, the possible reduction product of KMnO4, including MnO4 2− or Mn2+ ions, hardly affected the photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode (data not shown). Thus, a conclusion can be made that the decreased photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode by KMnO4 was ascribed to the oxidation of CDs by KMnO4, rather than through its reduction product of MnO2, MnO4 2− or Mn2+.

The different oxygen-containing functional groups modulated by the KMnO4 oxidation or AA reduction on the surface of the CDs can affect their surface states and largely influence the separation/recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [31], thus influencing the photocurrent generation of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode. When reacted with KMnO4, hydroxyls were oxidized to electron accepting carbonyls, which caused increased electron-hole recombination and inhibited photocurrent. Conversely, the introduction of the electron-donating hydroxyls on the surface of CDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs, and thus promoting the photocurrent generation. Consistently, the photo induced charge transfer of CDs under illumination was also manifested by the fluorescence response of the CDs after treated by KMnO4 and then by AA. That is, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was inhibited obviously after the addition of KMnO4, and the subsequent addition of AA resulted in an effective recovery of the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 2 ). This switched fluorescence of CDs in the presence of KMnO4/AA was consistent with that of the trend of the photocurrent variation of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode, which proved that the variation of the surface functional groups had a relationship with the recombination probability of electron-hole pairs and thus determined the photo induced charge transfer phenomena such as photoelectrochemistry and fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Split PEC immunoassay for CEA based on the switched photoelectrochemistry of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent responses of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode showed a linear range from 0.02 to 500 μmol/L (Fig. S3) and a low detection limit of 4.0 nmol/L (S/N = 3) was attained for AA. And the highly sensitive PEC response of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode to AA permitted us to establish a split-mode immunoassay. To realize this, we chose alkaline phosphatase (ALP), a critical hydrolase that having high turnover number and extensive substrate specificity in nearly all living organisms [32,33], as a catalytic tracer because ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis and transphosphorylation of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) to produce AA to restore the photocurrent of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs. Prior to conduct the immunoassay, we firstly validated that the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode enabled effectively probing the activity of ALP in a split-mode, in which the ALP-based enzymatic hydrolysis was conducted in a 96 microplate and then the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode was immersed in the hydrolysate's solution and then taken out for PEC measurement in the PEC detection cell. To rationalize the PEC detection for ALP, a series of control experiments were executed. The photocurrent of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode only revealed PEC response under the combined action of ALP and AAP rather than the ALP or AAP alone (Fig. S4A). Simultaneously, the photocurrent of the bare TiO2 was hardly affected by AA (Fig. S4B).

After optimized the experimental conditions for ALP detection (the results are displayed in Fig. S5), including the optimum reaction pH (8.5), reaction temperature (37 °C) and incubation time (20 min), we finally achieved the detection of ALP in the linear range from 0.005 to 150 U/L (Fig. 3 A and B) with the detection limit down to 0.0014 U/L (S/N = 3). Compare to the other methods for ALP detection, e.g. fluorescence and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), this detection limit is much lower (Table S1).

To exam selectivity of the detection system, the interference of different substances, including glucose oxidase (GOx), trypsin (Try), thrombin (Thr), BSA, K+, Na+, Ca2+, glutathione (GSH), and cysteine (Cys) on the signal responses of the FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode were investigated (Fig. 3C). The outcome implied that these potentially interfering substances did not cause obvious photocurrent change except for GSH and Cys, the similar reducing agents like AA. However, the influence of GSH and Cys can be inhibited with the help of N–ethylmaleimide (NEM), which can specifically reacted with sulfhydryl compounds [34]. On the basis of the preliminary results, we can confirm that the strategy showed good sensitivity and selectivity for ALP and then can be further applied as a general protocol for versatile immunoassays.

A novel split-type photoelectrochemistry was then employed for tracking a model antigen, CEA, a glycoprotein produced by colorectal cancer tissue that is used widely as a broad-spectrum tumor marker for clinical diagnosis [35–37]. Scheme 1 elaborated the principle of the PEC immunoassay system. In the 96 well microplates, the immobilized Ab1 bond to the antigen of CEA by the antigen-antibody specific binding and thereafter was integrated with the tag of Au NPs co-immobilized by Ab2 and ALP (Au NPs/Ab2/ALP) composite, whose successful synthesis was verified by the UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. S6). The ALP catalyzed the hydrolysis of AAP to AA and drove the surface state as well as the photocurrent recovery of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode. The photocurrent of the detection system enhanced linearly with the raised concentration of CEA (Fig. 4 A) over the range of 0.01 pg/mL to 30 ng/mL (Fig. 4B) with a quite low detection limit of 7.0 fg/mL. From Table S2, compare to the other methods for CEA detection, e.g. colorimetry, electrochemistry, electrochemiluminescence, and photoelectrochemistry, our detection limit won a landslide victory. The potential interfering species such as human IgG, goat IgG, alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), prostate specific antigen (PSA), BSA, human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), trypsin, thrombin, and lysozyme were assessed (Fig. 4C), which displayed that these interfering materials would not make a notable photocurrent variation. And hence we can see the satisfactory selectivity of the protocol.

The feasibility of the proposed split-type PEC immunosensing system for practical application was examined through applying it to CEA detection in real samples of human serums (from normal people) (Table 1 ). The results showed a good agreement with the commercially available ELISA kit with the acceptable recoveries over the range of 94.2–120.0%, indicating its potential for real sample applications.

This work displays a novel strategy to establish a split-type PEC immunosensor with the advantage of convenient, time/reagent-saving, sensitive and high throughput. The split-type immunoassay was exemplified by the detection of CEA (taken as a model) through a sandwich format using ALP as the catalytic tracer. The enzymatically generated AA obviously switched on the photocurrent of the KMnO4 treated FTO/TiO2/CDs electrode due to the formation of the electron donating hydroxyl groups to reduce the electron-hole recombination. This split-type immunoassay not only reduces the damage of the biomolecules by light irradiation or the destructive species produced by the irradiated photoelectrochemically active materials, but also it prevents the steric hindrance of the immobilized biomolecules on the photoelectrode to retard the signal generation. We hope this work may open up the development of strategies for split-type bioanalysis and further be extended for general enzyme-labeled PEC immunoassay for versatile targets.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575052, 21676123), the MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025), and the Opening Foundation of the Public Health Centre at Jiangnan University (JUPH201507).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.02.028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Acetylcholine esterase-labeled CdS nanoparticles on electrodes: photoelectrochemical sensing of the enzyme inhibitors
Quantitative photoelectrochemical detection of biological affinity reaction: biotin−avidin interaction
